title: Allocarya pygmaea
taxonomy: Allocarya pygmaea is classified within the family Boraginaceae, commonly known as the borage or forget-me-not family. This family consists of flowering plants that are primarily found in temperate and subtropical regions worldwide. As a member of this family, Allocarya pygmaea is a herbaceous perennial plant. The genus Allocarya itself is part of the tribe Cynoglosseae, which comprises plants that are often characterized by their unique flower morphology. Further classification details may require more specific research on the plant's taxonomy.

intro: Allocarya pygmaea, also known as Pygmy Waterleaf, is a small, perennial herbaceous plant native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat a variety of ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Allocarya pygmaea include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and sesquiterpene lactones, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Allocarya pygmaea is commonly used in herbal preparations such as teas, tinctures, and infusions, often in combination with other herbs to enhance its therapeutic effects.
